questions.question: If we compare the Earth to an onion, which characteristic of its layers is most accurate according to the NCERT text?

options.A: The layers are all of equal thickness.
options.B: It is made of several concentric layers with one inside another.
options.C: The layers are mixed randomly like a marble cake.
options.D: Only the outermost layer is solid.

questions.explanation: Just like an onion, the earth is made up of several concentric layers (layers with the same center) one inside another.

questions.question: The continental crust is 35 km thick, while the oceanic crust is only 5 km. What does this imply about the 'Sial' and 'Sima' layers?

options.B: The oceanic crust (Sima) is thinner but forms the base of the ocean basins.
options.C: Sial is found only at the core.
options.D: Sima is found only on mountain tops.

questions.explanation: The thin oceanic crust (5 km) is primarily 'Sima', while the thicker continental mass (35 km) is primarily 'Sial'.

questions.question: What is the specific volume distribution of the Earth's interior layers?

options.B: Crust 10%, Mantle 70%, Core 20%
options.D: Crust 15%, Mantle 84%, Core 1%

questions.explanation: The crust occupies only 1% of the volume, the mantle 84%, and the core makes up the remaining 15%.

questions.question: The term 'Nife' (Nickel and Ferrous) refers to the core. Why is the 'Ferrous' part significant?

options.A: It means the core is made of plastic.
options.B: It refers to the high iron content in the innermost layer.
options.C: It indicates that the core is made of aluminum.
options.D: It suggests the core is very cold.

questions.explanation: 'Fe' stands for Ferrous, which is the scientific name for Iron, one of the two main minerals in the core.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the formation of 'Extrusive' igneous rocks compared to 'Intrusive' ones?

options.A: Extrusive rocks have large grains; Intrusive have fine grains.
options.B: Extrusive rocks cool rapidly on the surface; Intrusive rocks cool slowly deep inside.
options.C: Extrusive rocks are made of fossils; Intrusive are made of sand.
options.D: There is no difference in their cooling rates.

questions.explanation: Rapid cooling on the surface creates fine-grained extrusive rocks (Basalt), while slow cooling inside creates large-grained intrusive rocks (Granite).

questions.question: How does the 'Rock Cycle' demonstrate the principle of conservation of matter?

options.A: Rocks are destroyed and never return.
options.B: One type of rock changes into another in a continuous cyclic manner.
options.C: The Earth is constantly getting smaller.
options.D: Rocks only change into liquid magma.

questions.explanation: The rock cycle is a continuous process where igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks transform into one another without 'disappearing'.

questions.question: Why are fossils primarily found in sedimentary rocks and not igneous or metamorphic rocks?

options.B: The high heat of magma and the pressure/heat of metamorphism destroy organic remains.
options.C: Animals do not like to die near volcanoes.
options.D: Only sedimentary rocks have the right color for fossils.

questions.explanation: Igneous rocks form from molten magma (too hot for life), and metamorphic rocks undergo changes that crush or melt fossils; sedimentary layers 'trap' and preserve them.

questions.question: The Latin word 'Sedimentum' means to settle. How does this relate to the formation of 'Sandstone'?

options.A: Sand grains settle, are transported, and then compressed into layers.
options.B: Sandstone is fire-born.
options.D: Sandstone only settles in the core.

questions.explanation: Sandstone is a sedimentary rock formed when grains of sand settle and are compressed/hardened into layers.

questions.question: Metamorphic rocks are the result of 'Metamorphose'. What triggers this change in Limestone to Marble?

options.B: The influence of great heat and pressure over time.
options.D: The action of wind.

questions.explanation: Igneous and sedimentary rocks change into metamorphic rocks when subjected to extreme heat and pressure.

questions.question: Which layer of the Earth would you find at a depth of 3500 km to 5000 km?

options.B: Mantle
options.D: Sial

questions.explanation: The mantle ends at 2900 km; beyond that is the core, which has a radius of about 3500 km.

questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between a 'Rock' and a 'Mineral'?

options.A: Rocks have a definite chemical composition; minerals do not.
options.B: Rocks are natural masses of mineral matter; minerals are substances with definite physical/chemical properties.
options.C: Minerals are only found in the core; rocks are only on the crust.
options.D: There is no difference.

questions.explanation: Rocks are aggregates of minerals, whereas minerals are specific chemical substances with distinct properties.

questions.question: Why can we not dig to the center of the Earth?

options.B: The heat and pressure are too high for any equipment or human to survive.
options.D: The center is made of water.

questions.explanation: The extreme temperature and pressure conditions in the mantle and core make it technologically impossible to dig 6000 km deep.

questions.question: If you find a rock with a very smooth, fine-grained texture, it is most likely:

options.B: Extrusive igneous rock
options.D: The core

questions.explanation: Extrusive igneous rocks (like Basalt) cool quickly on the surface, which results in a very fine-grained structure.

questions.question: In the rock cycle, what must happen to metamorphic rock before it can become igneous rock again?

options.A: It must be broken into sediments.
options.B: It must melt into molten magma.
options.C: It must be carved into a monument.
options.D: It must stay in the crust.

questions.explanation: For any rock to become 'igneous', it must first melt into magma and then cool/solidify.

questions.question: The 'Crust' is broken into plates. According to the depth mentioned, where would the boundary between the Crust and Mantle be found?

options.B: At 35 km under the continents and 5 km under the oceans.
options.D: At the center of the earth.

questions.explanation: The crust's depth is 35 km on land and 5 km under the ocean floor; the mantle starts immediately after.

questions.question: Granite is an intrusive rock. Why does it have large grains?

options.B: It cools down very slowly deep inside the earth, allowing crystals to grow.
options.D: It is found only in South Africa.

questions.explanation: Slow cooling of magma within the crust allows for the formation of larger mineral grains.

questions.question: Which of these sequences represents the correct order of the 'Rock Cycle' following an initial volcanic eruption?

options.B: Magma -> Igneous -> Sedimentary -> Metamorphic -> Magma
options.D: Crust -> Mantle -> Core

questions.explanation: Magma cools to Igneous; Igneous breaks into Sedimentary; Sedimentary changes to Metamorphic; Metamorphic melts back into Magma.

questions.question: What happens to 'Slate' if it is subjected to even further extreme heat and pressure?

options.B: It might eventually melt into magma.
options.D: It turns into sandstone.

questions.explanation: Metamorphic rocks like slate, if continued to be heated, will eventually reach their melting point and become magma.

questions.question: Why are minerals like petroleum and natural gas called 'Fossil Fuels' in the context of this chapter?

options.A: Because they are found in the mantle.
options.B: Because they are formed from the remains of organisms trapped in sedimentary layers.
options.C: Because they are made of fire.
options.D: Because they are found in the core.

questions.explanation: Fuels like coal and petroleum are minerals that originate from organic matter preserved in rocks.

questions.question: The 'Basalt' found in the Deccan Plateau suggests that this area was once:

options.B: Subject to massive volcanic activity where lava cooled on the surface.
options.D: Made of pure granite.

questions.explanation: Basalt is an extrusive igneous rock formed from surface lava; its presence indicates past volcanic eruptions.

questions.question: How does 'Limestone' differ from 'Marble'?

options.A: Limestone is igneous; Marble is sedimentary.
options.B: Limestone is sedimentary; Marble is the metamorphic form of limestone.
options.C: Marble is found in the core; Limestone on the crust.
options.D: They are the same thing.

questions.explanation: Limestone changes into marble due to metamorphic processes (heat and pressure).

questions.question: The Earth's core has a radius of 3500 km. Is this larger or smaller than the Mantle's thickness?

options.B: Smaller (3500 km vs 6000 km).
options.D: Smaller (3500 km vs 5000 km).

questions.explanation: The Core radius is 3500 km, while the Mantle extends to a depth of 2900 km.

questions.question: What provides the 'definite chemical composition' of a mineral?

options.B: The specific arrangement and types of atoms within it.
options.D: The person who digs it up.

questions.explanation: Minerals are defined by their internal structure and chemical makeup, which remains consistent.

questions.question: In the context of 'Sima', why is Magnesium mentioned alongside Silica for the oceanic crust?

options.A: Because Magnesium makes the crust float.
options.B: Because those are the two dominant minerals found in the rocks under the ocean.
options.C: Because Magnesium is only found in water.
options.D: Because it makes the crust red.

questions.explanation: The oceanic crust's chemical profile is predominantly Silica and Magnesium (Si + Ma).

questions.question: Why is the Crust called 'The thinnest of all layers' if it is 35 km thick?

options.A: Because 35 km is very small compared to the 2900 km mantle and 3500 km core.
options.B: Because it is made of paper.
options.C: Because it is only 1 km thick in most places.
options.D: Because it is actually only 5 km thick everywhere.

questions.explanation: Proportionally, the crust is extremely thin compared to the vast depths of the mantle and core.
